data.title: 5 Stocks I'm Buying As The Economy Slumps Toward Stagflation
data.description:
I have to be honest with you.

There's no coherent throughline to the menagerie of topics that follow. Instead, this week's article is a veritable hydra of subject matters, each of which, I believe, is worthy of paying attention to.

In these weekly articles, I do my best to highlight important information for investors, especially dividend investors, to absorb and ruminate upon.

As usual, after the mish-mash of topics covered below, I'll also discuss my buy list, which includes 5 dividend growers but also a handful of attractively priced preferred stocks of investment grade-rated REITs.

Here's where we're going today:

  • The Fed's economic projections are morphing into a stagflationary outlook.
  • Why most of the negative impact of the tariffs has yet to be felt.
  • How Big Tech is driving the economy forward even in the midst of multiple headwinds.
  • Stock prices and earnings expectations are going up, so does that mean the market is more or less efficient?
  • A recent Lazard report finds, again, that unsubsidized renewables are cost-competitive with even the cheapest forms of conventional energy. I'm not worried about utility-scale renewables.
  • Some thoughts on my dividend growth stock buy list and a handful of REIT preferred stocks on my radar.

Onward!

Reading The Fed's Tea Leaves

The June Federal Reserve meeting delivered exactly what the market expected: no rate cut. In fact, the Fed now projects only two 25-basis point rate cuts in the second half of this year.

Interestingly, the Fed's other economic forecasts for the economy also shifted somewhat from their March meeting to their June meeting.

In short, the Fed now expects lower real GDP growth, higher inflation, and slightly higher unemployment for 2025-2026 relative to their March projections. They also project a slightly slower pace of rate-cutting over the next few years.

Fed Chairman Powell commented during the press conference Q&A that there have been some noticeable price increases for certain tariff-impacted goods that have already shown up in the data, but due to the nature of supply chains, most of the impact is still ahead of us.

A very recent study by researchers from Harvard University and the University of San Andres (in Argentina) compared real-time retail pricing data with country-of-origin information to determine the product-level effects of tariffs. Their findings are consistent with what almost any economist would have told you.

As of June 1st:

Our analysis reveals rapid pricing responses, though their magnitude remains modest relative to the announced tariff rates and varies by country of origin. Both imported and domestic goods are affected, suggesting broader pricing and supply chain spillovers.

... These findings underscore the wide-ranging impact of trade policy, which can influence retail prices far beyond the specific goods targeted by tariffs.

It is an ongoing study that will be updated over time to show the exact inflationary impact of the tariffs.

Most of the Tariff Impact Is Still to Come

Speaking of the tariffs, here's a friendly reminder that tariffs are a net negative for the economy in virtually every measurable way.

(Sometimes they are deemed necessary for geopolitical or national security reasons, but that doesn't make them a net positive for the economy.)

When it comes to the negative economic impact of the tariffs, the worst is yet to come. And those negative effects won't be confined to relatively minor price increases in a few unimportant corners of the economy. Whenever you disrupt the stasis of a market-driven economy with some government intervention, there are consequences, often unforeseeable ones.

Let's just look at the (far more muted) tariffs from Trump's first term as an example.

Prior to the initial implementation of tariffs in September 2018, there was a burst of economic activity and industrial production to front-run those tariffs. The same burst of activity occurred in the opening months of this year, as you can see below.

But after that short burst of growth in industrial production (which includes manufacturing but also mining and utility output), a distinct slump followed it. For most of 2019, U.S. industrial production was contracting.

U.S. manufacturing entered its own recession in 2019. Even before the pandemic began, manufacturing output was plummeting.

It isn't just the industrial side of the economy. You can see the effect across virtually the entire economy.

For example, take a look at how the year-over-year growth of nonfarm payrolls as well as both the manufacturing and services PMI indices reacted to the tariffs in 2018:

It is striking how clear the pivot was from the period before the tariffs to the period following the tariffs.

It's still early days, which has probably lulled a lot of market participants into complacency, but I see little reason why the effect of tariffs on the U.S. economy would be significantly better today than it was in 2018-2019.

Already, over the last few years and certainly the last several months, the same measurements of economic health have been pretty weak:

Labor force growth of ~1% is undeniably soft, although it would be fair to point out that the downward trajectory has been very gradual.

As for the Institute for Supply Management's survey data, it is interesting to see the pre-tariff manufacturing bump earlier this year followed by its current slide. The services PMI didn't even get a bump. It has been sliding all year and recently slipped into contractionary (<50) territory.

Some corroborating evidence for this survey data is TSA throughput, the 3-month average of which recently dipped into contraction for the first time in several years.

As you can see, this drop in air travel traffic isn't strictly due to people suddenly becoming cautious in the midst of scary headlines. It is the continuation of a multi-year trend.

My take on all of this is that the U.S. economy is in a somewhat fragile state right now, and most economic indicators are likely to get worse (due largely but not entirely to tariffs) before they eventually get better.

The Core Drivers of GDP Growth Today

Above, I mentioned that I see little reason to believe that relatively minor tariffs would cause an economic downturn in 2019 while far larger tariffs won't cause a downturn today.

But, to be fair, there is one major difference in the U.S. economy between 2019 and today, and it relates to our technology sector -- the crown jewel of the American economy.

In Europe, central bank policy rates are going down. The Swiss National Bank just cut its key rate back to zero for the first time since 2022. And yet, here in the U.S., interest rates remain stubbornly elevated (at least relative to the last decade and a half).

Why?

Part of the reason is slightly higher inflation expectations, as explained above.

But another reason is the fact that U.S. real GDP growth has remained remarkably resilient.

I would argue that service-based economies are less susceptible to cyclical swings than agriculture- or manufacturing-based economies. But there's more to it than that.

Despite tariffs and incendiary geopolitics, most economists, including Torsten Slok, chief economist of Apollo Global Management (APO), foresee only a slowdown this year instead of a recession.

Service-based, market-driven economies with decent demographics tend to be pretty stable, but the U.S. economy has arguably been better than stable. American economic growth has been the envy of the world.

I'd argue that the American innovation ecosystem broadly and AI-related capital expenditures specifically have been the core driver of growth over the last several quarters.

The use of AI tools like ChatGPT is accelerating at a rapid pace.

And that is pushing Big Tech hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta Platforms (META) to allocate ever greater levels of capital to grow the physical and digital infrastructure required to run these AI tools.

These four hyperscalers alone are now spending over $70 billion per quarter on capex, with most of that associated with AI buildout.

There have even been reports that Meta's Mark Zuckerberg has offered compensation packages upwards of $100 million to poach top AI talent from other companies like OpenAI.

The level of investment in this area is incredible.

In Q1, investments into IT equipment (in the present case, data centers) contributed an astounding 100 basis points to GDP growth.

That is a greater contribution than the quarterly peak during the Internet boom of the late 1990s and early 2000s.

So, between the stability of a service-based, market-driven economy and unprecedented levels of technology investment, U.S. GDP growth does seem likely to keep powering forward, albeit at a slower pace.

Unless the trade war situation re-escalates, it now appears as though the U.S. economy will either avoid a recession or experience only a shallow one.

Stock Prices And Expected Earnings Growth Going Up

Lots of folks are commenting on how surprisingly buoyant the stock market has been in the midst of all the economic risks and scary headlines.

The S&P 500's (SP500, SPY) forward P/E ratio has rebounded above one standard deviation higher than its 30-year average level of 17x:

It is interesting that the SPY bounced right off that +1 standard deviation level after its April selloff. But I'm no market technician, and I should quickly back away from that topic before delving any further.

You can see from the box within the chart above that by basically every valuation metric, the SPY is well above its 30-year average levels. Price to cash flow, for example, was 44% higher than its 30-year average at the end of May.

On the other hand, a higher valuation makes sense if earnings growth justifies it. And after the post-"Liberation Day" mark-down of earnings expectations, the analyst consensus projection for SPY earnings over the next 12 months has now rebounded back above its pre-"Liberation Day" peak.

Further complicating the situation is the fact that the Magnificent 7 (MAGS) mega-cap stocks account for an outsized share of this expected earnings growth relative to the rest of the market:

The delta between the Mag 7's earnings growth and that of the rest of the market is shrinking, but it is still pretty big.

And, perhaps appropriately, the Mag 7's price performance over the last year has outperformed that of the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) by about the same amount as its expected earnings growth outperformance.

While the market is never perfectly efficient, it does appear to be efficiently valuing the Mag 7 relative to the rest of the market, at least assuming the analyst consensus earnings estimates are accurate.

So, maybe the market isn't that overvalued? Maybe the market is appropriately valuing the world's best innovation machines?

Okay, maybe Big Tech companies really are that awesome. Let's assume so, for the sake of argument.

How does that explain the rich valuation of the rest of the market outside the Mag 7 (or, in the chart below, the top 10 largest stocks)?

As you can see, all stocks outside of the top 10 largest are valued at about 19% above their 30-year average level.

Maybe the market anticipates that the users of AI (everything outside of Big Tech) will eventually be winners from this new technology as they incorporate it into their businesses and become more efficient and profitable.

Or maybe the world's affluent simply have tons of investable cash and see no better place to allocate it than US stocks.

Or maybe, as Josh Brown of Ritholtz Wealth Management has argued on his YouTube channel, American companies have become better managed and more profitable than ever before and therefore deserve higher valuations.

All of these arguments are logical in and of themselves.

But one should also consider the market's very long history of overshooting in one direction or another.

You know what they say about reversion to the mean.

Yes, Renewable Energy Is Cost-Competitive Without Subsidies

Details about the U.S. Senate's version of the congressional tax-and-spend bill recently emerged and caused quite a stir among green energy stocks, especially solar companies (TAN).

The House of Representatives version of the bill rapidly phased out tax credits for all aspects of green energy (development, production, and manufacturing), requiring projects to start within 60 days of bill signing and be completed by the end of 2028. The Senate's version of the bill slightly modified these provisions in a way that, to my mind, makes more sense while still phasing out subsidies fairly quickly.

In the Senate's modification of the bill:

  • The residential solar tax credit of 30% will expire 180 days after the passage & signing of the bill.
  • Solar and wind power have a longer runway to begin construction and still receive the tax credits: 100% if construction begins in 2025, 60% in 2026, 20% in 2027, and 0% in 2028 or after. This pulls forward and speeds up the expiration of the tax credits from the IRA, which wouldn't have been phased out until 2032.
  • New battery storage, nuclear, hydropower, and geothermal plants still have full tax credits through 2033, but the credits will then be phased out from 2034 to 2036.
  • Existing hydropower plants did not receive an extension of tax credits, which could have a detrimental effect on older dams that are in need of maintenance and upgrades.
  • Tax credits for new and used EVs would end six months after the signing of the bill.
  • Critically, the tax credits remain transferable, which helps developers with the upfront financing of new projects.

It is significant that the Senate's modification establishes construction start deadlines rather than in-service deadlines because renewable energy developers have limited control over when completed projects are connected to the grid and begin commercial operations. There are often long interconnection queues that make it difficult to predict exactly when a project will be placed into service.

The Senate bill looks very bad (worse than expected) for residential solar (hence the sudden plunge in many solar stocks).

But I'd argue that utility-scale renewables like wind, solar, and battery storage are going to be just fine. Growth will probably be slower than if the tax credits remained, as developer financing will become incrementally more expensive after this year. But renewables can and will still play a valuable role in meeting the country's growing power needs going forward.

The most recently released Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) report from Lazard provides more support for this thesis, as it finds new-build renewables cost-competitive with marginal use of conventional sources of power generation like gas and nuclear.

To quote the executive summary of the report:

On an unsubsidized $/MWh basis, renewable energy remains the most cost-competitive form of generation. As such, renewable energy will continue to play a key role in the buildout of new power generation in the U.S. This is particularly true in the current high power demand environment, where renewables stand out as both the lowest-cost and quickest-to-deploy generation resource.

It is true that renewables' LCOEs have rebounded slightly in recent years as a result of both higher capital costs and increased domestic sourcing of inputs (as opposed to cheaper international suppliers).

But as the management of NextEra Energy Inc. (NEE) has been attesting on their last few earnings conference calls, the construction and capex costs of conventional power plants have risen considerably in recent years as well.

Quoting the Lazard report again:

[W]hile this year’s analysis shows only a slight increase in the LCOE of CCGTs, turbine shortages, rising costs, and long lead times are expected to drive steep LCOE increases for gas technologies in the near term, as illustrated herein.

On the other hand, Lazard's report does find that the increased penetration of intermittent renewables in the grid is causing the cost of dispatchable power to increase, which limits the extent to which intermittent sources' share of power generation can or should rise.

Again quoting the report:

Despite the sustained unsubsidized cost competitiveness of renewable energy, resource planning metrics indicate diverse generation fleets will be required over the long term to meet power needs, likely bolstered by now-emerging technologies such as long-duration energy storage, geothermal, nuclear small modular reactors, pumped storage hydropower and carbon capture and storage, among others.

Like it or not, we are living in an "all of the above" energy world. The economy's needs cannot be sufficiently met by either fossil fuels or renewables on their own.

That's bad news for both pro-decarbonization and anti-renewables folks, but I'd argue it's good news for my fellow shareholders of renewable power producers like NextEra Energy, Brookfield Renewable (BEP, BEPC), and Clearway Energy Inc. (CWEN, CWEN.A).

My Dividend Stock Buy List

Without further ado, let's get to my current buy list, which is really two buy lists -- one made up of dividend growers and another of REIT preferred stocks.

First, the dividend growers:

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.3% Mid- to High-Single-Digits
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.3% Mid- to High-Single-Digits
Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) 4.1% Mid-Single-Digits
Public Storage (PSA) 4.1% Mid-Single-Digits
Rexford Industrial (REXR) 4.7% Mid-Single-Digits


Four of these 5 stocks were on my buy list from last week's article, titled "Why I Will Never Retire," so to avoid repetition, I'll point you to that article for my thoughts on these excellent REITs. The 5th company, Public Storage, is much like its other four REIT brethren on this list in that it boasts a market-leading position in its space, a fortress balance sheet, a high credit rating, and a long history of steady compounding.

That's the core of my investment strategy: Invest in high-quality dividend growers capable of steadily and reliably compounding cash flows indefinitely.

I try to studiously avoid both high-yielders and high-upside plays, and I'm suspicious of any stock that would need to grow at a 15% annual rate (or more) for the valuation to make sense.

A moderate yield and moderate growth at a reasonable (or better) price is my sweet spot. That's the most reliable way to generate long-term wealth, in my opinion.

I think PSA sold off recently in sympathy with homebuilders (XHB) and homebuilder-adjacent stocks like Home Depot (HD) after a poor homebuilder sentiment reading. Like HD, a lot of PSA's core customer demand comes from normal levels of housing turnover, which we currently do not have.

I don't know how long the current housing market malaise will last or how it will end, but I'm pretty confident that housing turnover (people moving from one place to another, which typically coincides with demand for storage units) will rebound eventually.

And now, here are the REIT preferred stocks I'm interested in buying:

Dividend Yield Upside To Par
Digital Realty 5.2% Preferred Series L (DLR.PR.L) 6.5% 24%
Regency Centers 6.25% Series A (REGCP) 6.7% 8%
Rexford Industrial 5.625% Series C (REXR.PR.C) 6.7% 18%

To be clear, I don't think upside to par matters that much because the DLR and REXR prefs are unlikely to be called anytime soon, while the REG pref could be called if interest rates decline somewhat, making it worthwhile to swap the 6.25% yield for a long-term bond at a lower interest rate.

You can find higher-yielding preferred stocks, including from REITs, but I'd argue that the risk-reward profile of these three REIT prefs is attractive. All three are investment-grade-rated REITs with relatively low leverage, conservative financial management, decent-or-better growth prospects, and high-quality property portfolios.

  • DLR owns and develops data centers, including some leased to hyperscalers.
  • REG owns Class A, mostly grocery-anchored shopping centers in prime locations across the country.
  • REXR owns highly functional infill industrial buildings in the supply-constrained region of Southern California.

Generating 6.5-6.7% yields from such strong and reliable REITs strikes me as a compelling opportunity.

Plus, each of them is trading near their lowest prices since their respective IPOs:

As you might have noticed from the images above, trading volume is something to keep in mind. During the last trading day, REXR.PR.C saw a grand total of 616 shares trade hands.

I certainly plan to use limit orders if and when I buy these prefs.

Over the next week, however, my incoming cash will be limited, so I may not be able to actually purchase any of these REIT prefs until the following weeks.

In any case, they are on my radar, so I thought I would share the ideas with those hunting for some safe yield.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - June 2025
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOG POST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P 500 (SP500) pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hyper-growth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

The market has continued to gain momentum this past month, building on the gains in the previous 30 days. The S&P 500 crossed the psychological threshold level of 6000, though it was previously achieved back in Nov. 2024.

Obviously, many things have happened between now and early April. Back then, the market panic was about tariffs and the disruptions they may bring. However, that tariff fear has more or less evaporated as the Trump administration has shown that it is mostly using tariffs as a negotiation tactic and intends to be flexible when needed. That said, it is likely that a base tariff of 10% would stay almost on every country, but that may not be such a bad thing and, in fact, may help in bringing down the trade deficit in the long run. Even though only one country (the U.K.) has finalized the trade deal so far, many others are progressing. The Trump administration has indicated that it is open to extending the 90-day pause for those where a deal is being worked out in good faith.

On the geopolitical front, the old wars are still raging, and a new one has just started between Israel and Iran. Obviously, the oil prices (CL1:COM) have shot up, but other than that, the market has taken it in its stride for now. Also, so far, the U.S. has stayed away from direct involvement, which is a positive for the market. However, that can change at any moment.

The Fed is still holding rates steady between 4.25% and 4.50%, at least until now (as of Jun.17). Most market participants (nearly 99%) see the Fed doing nothing and staying pat during its next FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) meeting on Jun. 18th. The earliest rate cut is expected in Sep. or Oct. 2025. In our view, the Fed is behind the curve and late in cutting rates as usual. Inflation is tame, and the relatively high interest rates are causing a lot of problems for the broader economy, especially for housing. However, it is entirely possible that the Fed will surprise the market with a 25-basis point rate cut on Jun. 18th, which will provide a big boost to the market as well as to strapped-up consumers.

You can see the odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

We can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. However, we intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score."

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, AVGO, CI, CTAS, CTRA, FIX, MS, and NRG (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: HWM (2 duplicates).

We are now left with 40 (50 - 10) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes DKS, MOS, QSR, CI, and VST (leaving us 35 names).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes OVV and SF, leaving us with 33 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. There is none on the list, leaving us still with 33 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major: We keep SAN but remove BK and ZION.

Finance – Inv. Banking and others: We will keep MS but remove MA and V.

Computer Software: We will keep ORCL and INTU but remove MSFT and MSCI.

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO but remove TSM.

Industrial- Const. & Engg.: We keep PWR.

Industrial- Aerospace & Def.: We keep HWM but remove CW.

Gold: We keep RGLD.

Utility: We keep NRG and NFG.

Energy: We keep IMO but remove CTRA.

REITs: We remove AMH and EQIX.

We also remove some of the stocks with the lowest relative strength in the list. We also remove MLI and LLY.

In all, we removed eleven names, so we are finally left with 20 (33-13) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(FIX), (SAN), (INTU), (ORCL), (RL), (PWR), (APH), (CF), (MS), (HWM), (RGLD), (MCK), (WPM), (IMO), (WING), (AVGO), (PM), (CTAS), (NRG), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is "not" a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (Jun. 2025):

(FIX), (SAN), (ORCL), (PWR), (APH), (HWM), (WPM), (AVGO), (PM), and (NFG).

Previous Month Selections:

(CTVA), (BCS), (MSFT), (STN), (APH), (HWM), (RGLD), (MCK), (AVGO), and (NRG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. However, this month again (similar to last month), a record-breaking 70% of stocks (7 out of 10) from the previous month are changing to new ones.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Past Performance

After a 12.5% gain last month, this month again, our portfolio had a strong showing with over 7% gain compared to 2.57% for the S&P 500.

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P 500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from Jan. 2010 until May 2025. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 15 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF, and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of Jan. 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

The portfolio had a very strong showing this month, gaining 7.23%, nearly 4.5% ahead of the S&P 500. The S&P 500 and our benchmark ETF, VIG, gained 2.57% and 3.01% in the same period, respectively. The star performers for this past month were Stantec (STN), Corteva (CVTA), and Amphenol (APH), closely followed by Broadcom (AVGO), Howmet Aerospace (HWM), and Royal Gold (RGLD).

This month, a full 70% of the stocks are being replaced, and we are retaining only three stocks from the previous month. The new entrants are Comfort Systems (FIX), Banco Santander (SAN), Oracle (ORCL), Quanta Services (PWR), and Philip Morris Int'l (PM). Also, National Fuel Gas (NFG) is replacing NRG Energy (NRG), while the gold royalty co, RGLD, is being replaced with Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM).

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not buy recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially at the present time. We recommend due diligence.

data.title: Cimpress: Attractive Valuation, But Catalysts Remain Unclear
data.description:
Introduction

Cimpress (NASDAQ:CMPR) is one of those companies where things are not clearly good or bad. After COVID, the business recovered well. Earnings came back, debt was managed, and operations returned to some stability. There is something real here—a functioning platform, competitive positioning, reasonable scale in a not-so-glamorous market. So far, so good.

But then the recent performance—it is not inspiring. Growth is slowing down, margins are under pressure, and three quarters in a row have been soft. Not disastrous, just underwhelming. At the current price, it is easy to say the stock looks cheap. But cheap does not always mean buyable. If there is no clear momentum, it can stay cheap for a long time.

Right now, I continue to hold. If it drops further, maybe I reconsider. But in the current setup, the best position is to observe and wait. The market is not missing the story—it just doesn’t have a reason to care yet.

Business Overview

The structure of the company has not changed. Cimpress owns several custom printing brands: Vistaprint, BuildASign, Pixartprinting, National Pen, and others. If you have ordered a sign, mug, or business card online, you’ve probably used one of their platforms without noticing. The business model is not innovative, but the execution over many years has built some barriers.

Their customer base is mostly small businesses. Volume is high, but the average order value is low. Every job is different, so operational efficiency must come from software and automation. Cimpress built its own system to handle this—called the Mass Customization Platform—and this is probably the most valuable part of the company. It handles batching, pricing, routing, and design automation.

They spend 10–15% of revenue on technology and engineering, which is a lot for a company that makes physical goods. But it makes sense—they are trying to automate something traditionally very manual. This is where they can defend margin and offer speed at scale.

Also, production is decentralized. Manufacturing is located regionally—Europe, U.S., Canada. That reduces shipping cost and FX exposure, and makes delivery faster. It also makes the system more resilient in theory, but more complicated to manage in practice.

This model works well in categories like signage and promotional items. Cimpress is probably best-in-class there. But as they expand into new verticals, complexity increases. Too many brands, too many websites. Managing all this is not easy, and the benefits of scale don’t always show up in the financials.

Financials

Fiscal 2024 looked encouraging. Revenue grew 7%, reaching $3.29 billion. Net income was $173.7 million, compared to a loss of $186 million the year before. VistaPrint, the largest business unit, grew 8% and posted over $328 million in segment EBITDA. National Pen and the others also returned to profitability. SG&A improved, and they refinanced their debt to 2032. Share buybacks resumed. Overall, a good step forward.

Unfortunately, that positive trend did not continue into FY 2025. The first quarter (July–September) was mixed. Revenue grew 6%, but free cash flow turned slightly negative. Capex increased, and working capital swung unfavorably. Adjusted EBITDA declined because of more investment in tech and marketing.

Second quarter (October–December) was weaker. Revenue growth was only 2%, and adjusted EBITDA dropped to $132.3 million from $166.5 million last year. Management explained that it was a tough holiday season, plus a postal strike in Canada and a tax charge related to Ireland. VistaPrint sales still grew 3%, but advertising costs pushed down margin.

Q3 (January–March) didn’t offer much relief. Total revenue increased just 1% year-over-year (3% on an organic basis), but adjusted EBITDA declined again. The company posted a net loss of $8 million, driven by foreign exchange losses and a non-cash impairment. VistaPrint held up reasonably well, but performance from the other business units remained soft. Free cash flow was once again negative, mostly due to seasonal working capital swings and higher capital expenditures. As a result, leverage climbed to 3.3x — not alarming yet, but definitely heading in the wrong direction.

So the trajectory: up in FY 2024, then sideways or slightly down so far in FY 2025. Not a collapse, but not what you want to see if you’re trying to argue for a re-rating.

It’s a frustrating setup. FY 2024 gave the bulls something to work with — real earnings, margin expansion, debt term pushed out, and capital returns. But FY 2025 so far feels like dead money. Revenue’s still inching up, but margins are getting squeezed, and free cash flow flipped negative right when they needed to prove consistency. Some of it is explainable (one-offs, FX, seasonality), but it’s not the slope you want if you're pitching this as a compounder. Leverage ticking up again doesn’t help. If Vista keeps comping low single digits and the other segments can’t offset, it’s hard to argue for multiple expansion unless they show a real cost discipline reset in the back half. Not broken — just muddled.

Valuation

The stock is not expensive. P/E is ~6x. EV/EBITDA is ~6.8x. Price-to-sales is under 0.35x. These are low numbers for a business that is still profitable, still generating cash, and has a tech backbone.

Compared to e-commerce or software-enabled peers (15x–25x EBITDA), Cimpress trades at a big discount. Even traditional printing companies usually trade closer to 10x. So yes, it looks cheap. But the reason is clear—growth has slowed, margins are unstable, and debt is still a heavy load.

Total debt is $2.2 billion. The notes due 2032 pay 7.375%, which is expensive. That kind of interest burden limits flexibility, even if maturities are pushed out.

Barrington cut their target from $95 to $65, though they still rate the stock “Outperform.” Most fair value estimates now sit around $42–$45. The market isn’t ignoring the story, but it is cautious. And for now, probably justifiably so.

Company Ticker Market Cap ($B) EV/EBITDA P/E P/S Notes
Cimpress plc CMPR 1.0 6.8x 6.0x 0.33x High debt; stabilized earnings
Deluxe Corp DLX 0.6 7.5x 9.8x 0.60x Traditional check printing & services
Cimpress (5yr average) CMPR — 10.3x 14.0x 0.60x Historical average pre-COVID
Vista Outdoor VSTO 1.2 5.7x 8.0x 0.35x Not a direct comp but prints branded gear
Shopify SHOP 90+ 70x+ — 10x+ E-comm infra; included to show tech spread
Avery Dennison AVY 17.5 12.2x 20.1x 1.35x Labels, industrial printing
Vistaprint (2009–2012) CMPR — 12–15x 18x+ 1.0x+ When it was a growth darling

Technical Setup

The stock chart is not encouraging. CMPR trades below both its 50-day and 200-day moving averages—$43.3 and $45.2, respectively. That’s a bearish setup. The RSI is around 44.0, so slightly oversold, but not dramatically. MACD is negative. Volume is weak.

Since the last earnings report, no clear buying has appeared. Price action is drifting—slowly down, occasionally flat, but no sign of strength. There is no real base forming, no clear support level. Maybe around $38, or $35, but these are guesses.

Without a positive surprise—either operational or macro—the technicals suggest more sideways movement or slow bleeding. It’s not in free fall, but it’s not setting up for a rally either. For now, the chart confirms the fundamental story: stuck.

Catalysts

Some things could improve the situation.

Cimpress is investing in AI tools—design generation, predictive logistics, customer service. If those reduce costs or improve productivity, margins could recover even with flat revenue. But it’s too early to say if these projects will deliver.

Also, if macro conditions improve, it helps. Their customers are small and medium businesses. When those businesses grow more confident, they spend more on marketing, signage, events. If GDP trends up and inflation cools, Cimpress benefits.

They are also expanding product lines: larger signs, apparel, home items, and marketing services. These could lift average order value, especially within the VistaPrint customer base. But the company has been talking about this for a while, and results are mixed.

Pixartprinting’s U.S. facility is opening in 2025. It may improve North American fulfillment and grow share. Worth tracking.

Lastly, they bought back $53M in stock in FY 2024, and $20M more in Q1 FY 2025. This is helpful, but limited, given leverage at 3.3x. Balance sheet work should probably come first.

Risks

The business is not recession-proof. Their core customers—small businesses—pull back spending fast when things get uncertain. Demand can fall quickly, and it’s hard to forecast.

There is also real competition. Amazon offers print services. So do small shops and Shopify plugins. Cimpress has scale, but not exclusivity.

Their corporate structure is complicated. Multiple brands, different fulfillment systems, various regions. Integration efforts never seem fully finished. That creates cost inefficiency and execution risk.

Currency volatility, inflation in raw materials, and environmental or data regulation in Europe are all potential problems. Again, none of these risks are new, but they are real.

Conclusion

Cimpress is not broken. But it is not in motion either. The business has strengths: scale, technology, production footprint. It survived the pandemic and came back stronger. But now, it’s losing momentum. Revenue is flat, margins are down, and management hasn’t given investors a new reason to care.

The valuation looks attractive, but it’s probably fair until something changes. This could become a good buy if trends improve, but right now, the market is asking a reasonable question: what’s the catalyst?

Maybe that comes from AI productivity gains or stronger SMB demand. Maybe Pixart’s U.S. launch surprises to the upside. But that’s all “maybe” right now. In the meantime, it’s a well-built machine that’s idling. You don’t need to sell it, but you probably won’t regret waiting either. Something needs to break the pattern—until then, this is a decent business with no real urgency.

I continue to hold. If the chart weakens more or results surprise to the upside, maybe I re-evaluate. But as of now, it’s not broken enough to sell and not compelling enough to buy.

Rating: HOLD

data.title: Peabody Energy Corporation: A Business-Changing Acquisition
data.description:
Peabody Energy (NYSE:BTU) is a coal producing company based in the United States. The company previously operated acreage in Wyoming, Colorado, New Mexico, Missouri, Illinois, and Indiana. Peabody's production was primarily concentrated in thermal coal, which was exported to various countries. However, starting this year, they began mining acreage located on the east coast of Australia following the acquisition of Anglo American. This acquisition allowed them, as we will see below, to add metallurgical coal to the company's current production. The main advantage of this is the higher price at which metallurgical coal trades compared to thermal coal, which generates potential revenue increases.

Thesis

As I mentioned previously, Peabody completed its acquisition of Anglo American, a company that operated several metallurgical coal mines on the east coast of Australia, in 2025. The deal, valued at $2.05 billion upon completion, plus a deferred payment of another $750 million and a potential additional $550 million. Should the Grosvenor mine, closed last year following a fire, be reopened, Peabody agreed to pay an additional $450 million. Analyzing this deal, it appears that its main contribution is the transformation of a large part of Peabody's business model. While the company was previously almost entirely a thermal coal producer, the acquisition of the Australian mines opens the door to a broader increase in the proportion of metallurgical coal.

This is important when we consider the forecasted growth in demand for this type of coal as the steel industry continues to grow globally. It's worth noting that the two main inputs for steel production are iron ore and thermal coal. This trend is closely related to the potential increase in steel production. At this point, it's important to remember that both growth in steel production and metals like copper are good indicators of global GDP growth due to their highly versatile economic applications.

Another point I would like to highlight is the change in the company's production profile that the acquisition of the new mines in Australia, which belonged to Anglo America, generates for the company. As we can see, at the former Peabody, there was a strong predominance of PCI (Pulverized Coal Injection) coal, used in steel production. However, Premium HCC was practically not produced by the company. With the acquisition, the increase in the latter becomes significant, going from an insignificant figure to representing a potential 39% of Peabody's production.

This displaces other, less profitable types of coal and makes way for higher-quality production, which would allow for increased revenue given the higher profitability. This change in the company's production profile is significant and, in my view, could lead to value generation that is beneficial to shareholders.

To conclude this segment, I'm interested in highlighting another aspect of Peabody that, in my opinion, stands out as one of the company's greatest strengths. As can be seen in the graph below, the company's FCF generation is by no means negligible. Much of this is due to the fact that capital investments are low relative to revenue. This is because the business model is mature, and while mining is capital-intensive, the reality is that once the holdings are fully exploited, the cash flows in that regard drop to maintenance capital.

1Q 2025 Results

In this segment, I'd like to briefly review the company's latest results and compare them with those seen in 2024. This will allow us to better understand the environment in which Peabody is operating, as well as give us an idea of ​​possible future projections. To start, I would like to show Peabody's revenue in 1Q 2025, which totaled $937 million, which is slightly lower than the $983.6 million in 1Q 2024.

If we analyze EBITDA, we see that in 1Q 2025, the company reported a value of $144 million, which represents a 10.28% drop compared to the $160.5 million in 1Q 2024. As mentioned, this is due to a lower amount of revenue generated by the company as a result of the fall in international coal prices, both thermal and metallurgical. In fact, if we analyze pre-produced volumes, we see that in 1Q 2025 the total was 28.9 million tons, compared to 27.4 million in 1Q 2024. Now, if we annualize the current EBITDA values ​​and compare them to Peabody's EV of $1.41 billion, we see that the EV/EBITDA ratio is at 2.45x, which represents a value that in my opinion is extremely attractive, even with the year-over-year decline.

If I analyze Net Income, the drop was much greater considering that 1Q 2025 closed at a total of $34.4 compared to $39.6 in 1Q 2024, equivalent to a drop of 13.1%.

One point I'd like to highlight is the net cash provided by operating activities, which was $119.9 million in 1Q 2025, slightly higher than the $119 million in 1Q 2024. But beyond the slight growth, what I'd like to emphasize is that if we annualize the value and compare it to the company's market cap, we see that the P/CF ratio is equivalent to 3.44x, which implies that if these levels remain, the company will be able to generate cash equivalent to a market cap in three and a half years. This extremely low value, in my view, provides a margin of safety that I find difficult to dismiss.

Finally, I'd like to point out that the bulk of Peabody's debt is in Convertible Senior Notes paying an interest rate of 3.25%, totaling $320 million, while the total debt value amounts to $402.1 million. Considering the available cash of $696.5 million, we see that the company's financial structure is extremely sound, and in fact, it has the necessary margin to address future contingencies.

Valuation

For this section, I would like to conduct a valuation of the company using two different methods. First, I use a DCF model I built based on a series of assumptions that, in my opinion, are rather conservative. The idea behind the model's variables is to consider scenarios that are not entirely positive or extremely favorable for the company, so that a good margin of safety can be established when making the valuation. The second model is based on a valuation using multiples, using the main coal producing companies in the United States and Australia as comparables, given the company's current mining structure since the acquisition.

I'd like to start by clarifying the assumptions of the DCF model and then present the results obtained. To run the model, I used a 22% tax rate, which is above the average for recent years, as can be seen in the Financials segment of Seeking Alpha. Regarding the perpetual growth rate, given that these are companies with a mature business model based on the production of a good that is estimated to cease production in the coming decades, I used a value of 1%. This value is much lower than the ones I usually use for valuations of this type, but given the conservative model and the company's characteristics, I believe this approach is appropriate.

Regarding EBIT, my assumption was a decreasing increase over the first five years. This would be in line with the change in the business model proposed by the mines in Australia. The shift in production toward increased met coal instead of thermal coal, as I mentioned in the first part of the report, could help increase the company's revenue and therefore its EBIT. I wanted to reflect this in the model. I assumed an EBIT equal to that of 2024 for the first year, a 5% increase for the second year, 4% for the third, and 3% for the final two years. While these increases are in line with what could be expected from the acquisition, I think they are quite conservative given the company's projections.

With all these assumptions, it can be seen that Peabody's upside potential is truly interesting and, in my view, far from negligible. Given the conservative estimates I made, I think this valuation offers a good margin of safety, which gives me a good level of confidence regarding BTU's potential.

Australia. Given Peabody's conversion to a mixed thermal and metallurgical coal production model, I decided to change the SeekingAlpha peers and adapt them slightly to this new reality. For the comparison, I considered HCC, AMR, ARLP, and WHITF as other players. To avoid being limited to a single model, I sought to value the company using multiples.

To do this, I considered the major coal-producing companies in both the United States and Australia. Given Peabody's conversion to a mixed thermal and metallurgical coal production model, I decided to change the SeekingAlpha peers and adapt them slightly to this new reality. For comparison, I used HCC, AMR, ARLP, and WHITF as other players. Taking all this into account, it's worth noting that, given WhiteHaven's lack of positive PE in the most recent period, I decided to assign it a value of 20x to avoid overly weighting it but still penalizing the company's share price in that regard. With this in mind, it can be seen that BTU has a good upside margin that is comparable to the DCF model I proposed earlier. This gives me confidence that the company is truly undervalued relative to its potential value.

Risks

Turning to the risks, one of the main ones emerging is the one that the new administration could unleash on international trade in the United States. With Trump's return to the presidency, the White House is seeking to implement a policy that increases tariffs on American imports. Among the most affected countries is China. This is relevant since, as I mentioned in previous articles, China is the world's leading steel producer, with more than 50% of the global market share.

Therefore, the possibility of a trade battle between the United States and China over import tariffs between the two countries, to the point that GDP growth in both economies is affected, could impact coal demand in the short term. At this point, it's important to remember that coal and iron ore are basic inputs in the steel industry. Therefore, a slowdown in growth in the world's leading producer could undoubtedly have negative effects on Peabody.

Another point of contention is the possibility of expected short-term production cuts given current coal prices. Especially considering metallurgical coal prices, there is current concern that production quantities will tend to decline due to company cutbacks. This is because, at current prices, stocks and production levels are causing some concern.

Conclusion

To close this report, I would like to emphasize that the risks I mentioned previously are short-term risks, in my view. It is true that the world's energy matrix is ​​moving toward cleaner and more sustainable forms. The desire by stakeholders to wean the world off fossil fuels is a long-term reality. Still, as I pointed out in my thesis and in other articles I authored (for example, Alpha Metallurgical Resources: A Diamond Among Coals), current steel production methods and the growth of emerging economies like India lead me to believe that coal demand could remain at current levels for a long time.

To this, we must add the possibility of converting the company to a more lucrative business model, incorporating metallurgical coal extraction into its existing thermal coal production, could generate a more than significant increase in revenue. Considering the two valuations I showed, we can see that the company has more than attractive growth potential, combined with a cash level that allows it to cover the company's total debt. This could provide sufficient resilience to address potential short-term risks.

Finally, I would like to highlight that the valuation, combined with the company's current multiples and acquisitions, provides, in my view, a good margin of safety that makes the company a good buy opportunity for the future.

data.title: Nike Q4 Earnings Preview: The Generational Buying Opportunity Is Here
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Nike (NKE), I upgraded the stock to a cautious “buy,” as I believed the management was making strategic long-term decisions to reignite brand authenticity and momentum through newness and innovation, particularly in Performance Sports and shifting Nike Digital to a full-price model.

However, since then, the stock has declined over 24%, hugely underperforming the S&P 500.

The company reported its Q3 FY25 earnings in March, where revenue and profit margins declined even faster on a sequential basis. Yet, despite the deceleration, it managed to exceed analyst expectations. During the earnings call, the management emphasized its “Win Now” strategy by doubling down on its product portfolio, where it expects the growth from its new performance products to offset declines from its classic footwear franchise by FY26, with positive responses coming from their Pegasus Premium and Vomero 18 products.

Meanwhile, the company continued to make progress on building its integrated marketplace across Nike Direct and Wholesale, with the number of promotional days going down from 30 to 0 as they focused on building targeted sports-led content and storytelling for driving retention and higher average order value purchases.

Nike is slated to report its Q4 FY25 earnings on 06/26, where revenue and earnings are expected to decline even faster, with consensus estimates now projecting the deceleration to continue into full year FY26 before the company returns to growth from FY27 onwards.

The way I see it, I believe that Nike is fighting a tough battle bringing back optimism to its performance sports-focused product portfolio through storytelling, product innovation, and a go-to-market strategy built around Nike Direct and Wholesale channels. However, I believe that the company is indeed showing early signs of success, where the management is optimistic on the timeline where its new product innovation will gain sufficient scale as they endure through the short-term pain of inventory liquidation and margin pressures to build up the required capacity for long-term growth.

While the macroeconomic environment continues to be turbulent in Nike’s main markets, which include North America and Greater China, amid rising competition, I believe it is a generational buying opportunity with an attractive risk reward. As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating with a price target of $82 as we wait for further clarity and direction on its Q4 earnings call.

Q3 FY25 Highlights: Revenues And Profits Decline Even Further, Inflection Point Expected In FY26

Nike reported its Q3 FY25 earnings in March, where total revenue declined 9% YoY to $11.3B. Despite revenues declining at a faster pace compared to the previous quarter, the company exceeded consensus estimates by 2%.

Out of the $11.3B, Nike Direct revenues declined 12% YoY to $4.7B, driven by a 15% YoY decline in Nike Digital and a 2% decrease in Nike-owned stores. Meanwhile, its Wholesale revenues were down 7% YoY to $6.2B.

From a geographic standpoint, its North American market saw some signs of stabilization, with revenues declining at a slower rate sequentially at -4%, compared to -8% in the previous quarter, contributing close to 45% of Total Revenue. However, its Europe, Middle East & Africa along with Greater China markets, saw faster deceleration compared to the previous quarter.

In my previous post on Nike, I had discussed that the management was doubling down on Nike’s strengths, where they are committed to leading with sport and putting the athlete at the center of innovation, design, product, and storytelling in order to fuel their brand excitement and authenticity.

At the same time, I also discussed that one of the strategic actions of Nike is to build an integrated marketplace across Nike Direct and Wholesale by returning Nike Direct digital and physical to premium destinations with a full-price model and limiting promotions to traditional retail moments only, while simultaneously earning back trust with their wholesale partners to build their product pipeline and greater collaboration.

During the earnings call, the management updated their progress on their strategic priorities, which they call “Win Now,” that are focused on five fields of play (running, basketball, football, training, and sportswear), key countries (United States, China, United Kingdom), and influential cities (New York, Los Angeles, London, Beijing, and Shanghai).

Particularly, when it comes to their product portfolio, the management discussed their commitment and progress towards improving their brand and innovating across their five key fields of play, especially in training and running with Pegasus Premium and Vomero 18, although it has not yet proven to offset the headwinds from their classic franchises. At the same time, the company is also looking to diversify their sportswear apparel by reducing their reliance on fleece by launching their 24/7 collection that has seen a positive response from customers as they bring performance materials to training apparel.

What is also important to note is that when asked about the timeline for new performance products growth to offset the decline in classic footwear, the management guided for the inflection point in FY26, where new innovation would gain sufficient scale.

Meanwhile, on the topic of building an integrated marketplace in order to drive superior consumer-led experiences, the management highlighted that their progress in Nike Direct Digital will take shape more quickly, where, compared to January and February of last year, the number of promotional days went from 30 to 0 as they build targeted sports-led content and storytelling for driving retention and higher average order value purchases.

However, when it comes to profitability, Nike saw its gross and EBIT margins decline both on a year-over-year and sequential basis to 41.5% and 10.9%, respectively, as a result of higher liquidation of excess inventory through less profitable channels, rising product costs, and changes in channel mix.

What To Watch Out For In Q4 FY25?

The company is slated to report its Q4 FY25 earnings on 06/26, where consensus estimates point to revenue of $10.71B, which would represent a year-over-year decline of 15%, larger than in Q3. Meanwhile, analysts are expecting the company to post earnings per share of $0.12 during the quarter, which would represent a contraction of 87%.

In the meantime, forward consensus estimates have gotten further pessimistic on growth prospects on both the top and bottom lines since the time of my last writing, where they now expect revenue to turn positive from FY27 onwards. Note that this is more pessimistic than what the management hinted at, where they expect an inflection in FY26 from their renewed product portfolio offsetting the declines from their classic franchise.

The same is true for forward earnings per share estimates, which are expected to turn positive from FY27 onwards.

While beating both its top and bottom lines for the quarter will be viewed positively by investors, I believe that the main focus will be on the forward guidance as to whether the management guides higher than what the consensus estimates are currently pricing in.

Given that comps have become easier with negative revisions and Nike seeing some early signs of success with its product portfolio and its integrated marketplace as it right-sizes its inventory to usher in newness and momentum, a guidance higher than consensus estimates may lead to a sizable rerating of the stock, in my opinion.

Most importantly, I believe that investor sentiment will be highly dependent on the consistency of commentary across a) the pace of their product portfolio transition, where they are focused on increasing the contribution of newness to offset the declines from classic footwear franchises by 2026; b) the momentum in building their integrated marketplace for Nike Direct and Wholesale with early signs of revenue stabilization across both channels; and c) the state of inventory.

At the same time, the macroeconomic environment is turbulent with a mixed consumer demand landscape, particularly in North America and China, while competition is getting fiercer. As a result, it will be important to assess how the evolving macroeconomic landscape is impacting Nike’s progress to return its brand back into growth territory.

Revisiting My Valuation

Looking forward, I will take the consensus estimates for both revenue and earnings estimates to reassess my price target. When it comes to revenue growth, consensus estimates point to a year-over-year decline in FY25 and FY26, followed by a return to mid-single-digit growth from FY27 onwards as the company rightsizes its inventory with its renewed product portfolio along with a well-integrated marketplace across Nike Direct and Wholesale.

From a profitability standpoint, Nike is expected to post declines in earnings per share in both FY25 and FY26 before returning to growth in FY27 onwards as a result of easing comps, along with optimal inventory conditions. From FY29 onwards, consensus estimates point to earnings per share growing in the mid-teens range, which is roughly twice that of the average S&P 500 company.

Therefore, assigning a price-to-earnings ratio that is twice that of the average S&P 500 at 34, given the growth rate of its earnings, Nike should fetch a price target of $82 per share, representing an upside of 36% from its current levels.

My final Verdict And Conclusions

While beating both its top and bottom lines in Q4 will be favorable, the stock’s future direction is heavily reliant on management’s forward guidance. With the consensus estimates now pointing to yet another year of revenue deceleration in FY26, I believe the management may surprise investors to the upside should the momentum in its new product portfolio be sufficient enough to offset the declines from its classic franchise.

With the risk-reward looking even more attractive than at the time of my last writing, I will maintain my “buy” rating with a price target of $82 per share.

data.title: Altria Group: Buy This High-Yielding Dividend Star Now
data.description:
As a dividend growth investor and analyst, it's probably not a surprise to learn that I appreciate companies with a commitment to returning capital to shareholders. This is often done through a combination of growing dividends and share repurchases.

Such a characteristic requires strong business fundamentals to be done responsibly for long periods. After all, a company without adequate earnings and cash flow can't pay a dividend and do share buybacks without resorting to debt. It's often easy to spot when this is being done.

Altria Group (NYSE:MO) is a company that, I believe, has a clear culture of prioritizing long-term value creation for shareholders. When I last covered Altria with a buy rating in April, I liked the 55-year dividend growth streak that made it a Dividend King. I also thought expansion into non-nicotine adjacencies was a reasonable path to solidify its long-term growth picture. The investment-grade balance sheet was another aspect of Altria that I appreciated. Lastly, shares were undervalued by a double-digit percentage of my fair value estimate.

Today, I'm maintaining my buy rating. Altria continues to plan launches for a handful of non-nicotine adjacent market products over the next few years. These plans are complemented by its extensive distribution reach. Last month, S&P upgraded Altria's credit rating to BBB+ on a stable outlook. Shares remain priced below my revised fair value estimate.

I'm Still Cautiously Optimistic

On April 29th, Altria shared its first-quarter earnings report for the period ended March 31st, 2025. The company's revenue net of excise taxes decreased by 4.2% year-over-year to $4.52 billion during the quarter. That was $100 million less than Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter.

What was behind this top-line miss for the first quarter?

Altria's smokeable products segment generated $3.91 billion in revenue net of excise taxes during the first quarter. For more perspective, this was down 4.1% over the year-ago period in the quarter.

Growth of illicit e-vapor products and discretionary income pressures on adult tobacco consumers led reported domestic cigarette shipment volume to drop 13.7% year-over-year for the first quarter. Adjusting for trade inventory movements and calendar differences, volume was down about 9% during the quarter. This was only partially countered by price increases. That's why revenue net of excise taxes decreased by a mid-single-digit percentage rate in the quarter.

Altria's oral tobacco products segment reported $629 million in revenue net of excise taxes for the first quarter. This was a 0.5% increase over the year-ago period. Reported domestic shipment volume fell by 5% year-over-year during the quarter (including synthetic oral nicotine pouch products). Adjusting for calendar differences and trade inventory movements, shipment volume decreased by 1% in the quarter. Higher prices helped the segment to post modest topline growth for the quarter.

Altria's adjusted diluted EPS grew by 6% over the year-ago period to $1.23 during the first quarter. Improved operating efficiency helped the company's non-GAAP net profit margin to expand by over 280 basis points to 46.2% in the quarter. That fueled a 2.1% uptick in adjusted net income to $2.09 billion for the quarter. Along with a 3.9% reduction in the share count from share repurchases, this is how adjusted diluted EPS growth surged higher as revenue net of excise taxes declined during the quarter.

In the coming years, I believe that Altria has catalysts to keep adjusted diluted EPS moving higher.

First, on! nicotine pouch volume climbed 18% year-over-year to 39.3 million units in the first quarter. That brought Altria's share of the U.S. oral tobacco category to 8.8%. That was up 1.8 percentage points over the year-ago period.

In his opening remarks during the Q1 2025 Earnings Call, CEO Billy Gifford pointed to responsible brand-building investments as contributing to this growth. The new It's On! campaign resulted in consumer impressions of on! growing by almost 5x versus the prior year. This year, Helix plans to keep up this momentum by bringing the campaign to more consumers in new and existing channels.

Currently, awareness of the on! brand is over 60% among oral nicotine pouch consumers. As brand awareness continues to grow from marketing efforts, I believe that growth could accelerate further for the brand.

As I highlighted in my previous article, Altria plans on using its distribution leverage to selectively invest in and launch non-nicotine adjacency market products. The company is targeting five product launches with broad commercial distribution by 2028, including its investment in the Proper Wild differentiated energy shot brand.

On the regulatory front, Altria has been focused on advocating for regulatory reforms to speed up product authorizations and improve enforcement against illicit e-vapor products. For more color, illicit e-vapor products are now estimated to be over 60% of the category, eating into Altria's cigarette volumes.

Fortunately, some progress is being made in ramping up efforts to combat this issue. Gifford noted at the time of the earnings call that two states had enacted directory legislation in 2025 and another 21 states have pending legislation addressing the issue.

Altria has also been engaging with members of Congress and administrative staff to bring further attention to this issue. This is with the intent that the federal multi-agency task force created last year will take stronger, more coordinated action against illicit e-vapor.

For these reasons, I believe the FAST Graphs analyst consensus over the next few years is rational. In 2025, a 5.5% uptick in adjusted diluted EPS to $5.40 is the current consensus. For 2026, another 3% increase in adjusted diluted EPS to $5.56 is projected. In 2027, an additional 2.7% rise in adjusted diluted EPS to $5.71 is being forecasted.

Financially, Altria appears well-positioned for the future. As of March 31st, 2025, the company's total debt-to-EBITDA ratio was 2.1x. This was in line with its targeted leverage ratio of 2x. As a result, Altria's credit rating was upgraded from BBB on a positive outlook to BBB+ on a stable outlook by S&P (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to Altria's Q1 2025 Earnings Press Release and Altria's Q1 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Shares Appear Discounted

Altria's shares have delivered 18% total returns so far this year. That's significantly more than the 2% total returns posted by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

Even so, I would argue that Altria's shares are still undervalued. The stock's forward 12-month P/E ratio of 10.9 is moderately below its 10-year average P/E ratio of 13.8, according to data from FAST Graphs. To be clear, I believe a reversion to the 10-year average P/E ratio would go beyond its fair value.

Lower 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yields over the past decade that supported Altria's valuation multiple are likely not coming back anytime soon. The average yield of the 10-year in the past decade was about 2.5% per Macrotrends.

My updated fair value P/E ratio is now 12. This is a slight adjustment lower than my previous fair value multiple of 12.5.

That reflects my expectation that the 10-year yield in the years ahead will be in the mid- to high-3% range. This is a slight bump from my prior expectation of 10-year yields in the low- to mid-3% range.

In the past decade, the investment spread of Altria's earnings yield to the 10-year yield was around 475 basis points. Moving forward, I believe the recent credit rating upgrade by S&P balances the long-term uncertainty of its business model that remains.

That's why I'm comfortable still applying a 475 basis point spread to the 10-year Treasury yield. This is how I arrive at my fair value P/E ratio of 12.

Currently, the calendar year 2025 is approximately 47% complete. That means there's another 53% of 2025 and 47% of 2026 yet to come in the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $5.48.

Using my fair value P/E ratio of 12, I come out at a fair value of nearly $66 a share. Against the current $59 share price, this is a 9% discount to fair value.

If Altria grows as anticipated and reverts to fair value, it could post a 19% cumulative total return through June 2026. In the next five years, annual total returns would be just above 10%.

Another Dividend Increase Is Around The Corner

Altria's 6.9% forward dividend yield clocks in at more than double the consume staples sector median forward yield of 3%. This accounts for A+ grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively.

Long-term concerns over Altria's dividend viability are somewhat warranted, though. The company will eventually have to execute other growth avenues to offset its declining core business. However, the dividend is relatively safe over the medium term in my view.

That's because Altria's adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is positioned to be in the high 70% range for 2025. This would be better than the 85% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry, according to The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal.

Additionally, the free cash flow payout ratio in Q1 2025 was near 65%. That was up a bit from the low-60% range in Q1 2024 but is still healthy dividend coverage. Overall, this is why I think the Quant System's C+ grade for overall dividend safety is probably fair if not a bit low.

Due to these payout ratios, I believe that Altria is primed to declare its 56th straight year of dividend growth later this summer. My best guess is that the company will up the quarterly dividend per share by 3.9% to $1.06. That's head and shoulders above the sector median of just 2.8 years.
