This effortlessly earns Altria an A+ grade for overall dividend consistency from the Quant System.

The Quant System's annual forward dividend per share growth projections of 4.2% line up with my expectations as well. That warrants a C grade for the metric.

Risks To Consider

Altria's continued growth from on! and the recent S&P credit rating upgrade are positives. Yet, the company has risks that could hurt or even break the investment thesis.

Altria is facing a couple of factors that are impacting its reported domestic cigarette shipment volumes that are worth watching for further deterioration.

For one, illicit e-vapor products from China are increasingly pressuring smokeable product volumes. It looks as though there is some movement in the right direction to address this issue. If that doesn't end up being the case, though, there's the risk that Altria's volume declines could remain elevated or even accelerate.

After the impacts of cumulative inflation since coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic, adult tobacco consumers still face discretionary income pressures. Any further loss of purchasing power (e.g., wage growth not keeping pace with inflation) could intensify these concerns. That could lead more consumers to switch from premium brands like Marlboro to discount cigarettes, which could squeeze profit margins.

In the long term, the risk remains that on! and upcoming non-nicotine adjacent market product launches won't be enough to sustain its business. If this proves to be true, the investment thesis would be undermined.

Summary: A Dividend King With Solid Total Return Potential

Altria remains a reliable income stock. I still think that the company's risks are balanced out by the growth opportunities that its distribution power affords it. Altria's credit rating upgrade by S&P on a stable outlook is a bonus. Shares are also cheap enough that I believe double-digit annual total return potential is likely from here. This is why I'm reiterating my buy rating.

data.title: Lamb Weston: Positioned For A Comeback As Volumes Recover And Cost Initiatives Gain Traction
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While Lamb Weston Holdings, Inc. (LW) faces near-term headwinds from a lost QSR customer in the U.S. and soft global restaurant traffic, the company’s revenue growth is poised to recover with volume momentum building, innovation gaining traction, and price pressure easing. The company has fully restored volume lost during last year’s ERP transition and is adding incremental business through new customer wins across both domestic and international markets. This, along with new product introductions and easing pricing pressures, should help revenues beyond FY25.

On the margin front, while there are some near-term pricing pressures, lower raw material costs, especially for potatoes, should partially offset this headwind. Beyond FY25, the margins should benefit from operating leverage as the sales recover. The company is also targeting cost savings through SG&A reductions, reducing warehouse space, etc., which should drive margin expansion. In terms of valuation, the stock is trading at a discount to its historical averages. This, coupled with the improving revenue and margin growth prospects, makes me positive, and I have a buy rating on LW stock.

Revenue Analysis And Outlook

LW’s sales have seen some pressure over the last year due to the soft global quick service restaurant (QSR) traffic trends, as soaring prices and economic uncertainty pressured consumer spending. Further, in Q3 2024, the company faced some difficulties in transitioning to the new ERP system, which in turn resulted in lower order fulfilment rates, customer losses, and a decline in market share.

However, Q3 2025 showed a recovery in the sales of the company as it absorbed the prior-year effect of cancelled shipments related to the ERP transition last year. This recovery was additionally backed by the regaining of shares of regional and small customers lost in the preceding year and incremental volume from recent chain customer contract wins. As a result, the company’s sales increased by 4.3% Y/Y to $1.521 billion.

Segment-wise, the North-American segment’s sales increased by 4.1% Y/Y, driven by an 8% growth in sales volume from the full restoration of the lost customer volume from the previous year and benefits from new contract wins across all channels. The volume gains were partially offset by sluggish restaurant traffic trends. Price/mix declined 4% because of the impact of planned investments in price to attract and keep volumes in the ever-increasing competitive environment, partly mitigated by favorable channel and product mix.

The International segment showed a sales growth of 4.6% Y/Y, with a 12% increase in sales volume stemming from the recent customer contract wins and lapping unfulfilled orders due to the prior year’s ERP transition. Price/mix declined by 7% due to strategized pricing actions to fuel customer wins and volume gains in the highly competitive environment and unfavorable FX translation. On a constant currency basis, price/mix declined by 4%.

While the macro backdrop remains challenging and global restaurant traffic continues to slide under the weight of stretched consumer budgets, there are signs of stabilization on the horizon. U.S. inflation came in softer than expected in April and May, and consumer sentiment inched higher in June. For Lamb Weston, that’s an encouraging sign as the company looks to regain its footing after several quarters of pressure.

Even with traffic down, one important metric that has held up is the “French fry attachment rate,” which remains nearly two points above pre-pandemic levels. That stickiness suggests fries are still a priority on the plate and bodes well for a demand rebound once macro conditions improve.

Operationally, Lamb Weston is already turning a corner. The company has fully replaced volume lost during last year’s ERP transition and is adding incremental business through new customer wins across both domestic and international markets. A major highlight is the company’s new contract with a fast-growing QSR chain that is shifting to frozen products, with a full national rollout expected through late 2025 and into 2026. Private-label expansion in grocery and club channels is also gaining traction, with strong early sell-through.

Product innovation is another lever driving growth. In North America, new lines like battered and seasoned fries and “fridge-friendly” products that keep for up to seven days without freezing are expanding the company’s reach, especially among operators with limited cold storage. Retail is also getting a lift from expanded offerings such as onion rings and cheesy potato bites. Internationally, the 3-sided Frenzy Fries or the “reimagined classic Fry” has landed well with customers and could become a good revenue contributor as distribution scales.

On pricing, there’s still pressure, but it appears to be easing. The 5% price/mix decline in Q3 reflected strategic pricing actions in both North America and international markets in response to increasing competition, but trends are stabilizing. For Q4, price/mix is expected to fall in the low- to mid-single-digit range (management guidance), with North America continuing to absorb competitive pricing and mix headwinds tied to weaker traffic. International price/mix, however, is expected to be roughly flat on a constant currency basis, an encouraging shift from earlier declines and a sign that competitive dynamics are leveling out.

There are some near-term headwinds with Q4 sales reflecting the impact of a lost QSR customer in the U.S., and overall traffic remaining soft. However, with new national QSR client ramping, innovation gaining traction, and price pressure abating, the setup for fiscal 2026 looks positive. Add to that a potentially improving macro environment with potential trade policy clarity, inflation data coming positive, and consumer confidence improving, Lamb Weston appears well-positioned for a revenue growth recovery.

Margin Analysis And Outlook

Margins at Lamb Weston were a mixed bag in Q3 2025. Adjusted gross margin fell 170 basis points year over year to 27.6%, weighed down by price and trade investments, higher transportation and warehousing costs, and increased depreciation tied to recent capacity expansions. But that pressure was partially offset further down the P&L. Adjusted SG&A as a percentage of sales improved by 100 basis points, thanks to the company lapping last year’s ERP transition costs and realizing savings from its ongoing restructuring efforts.

As a result, adjusted EBITDA margin edged up 30 basis points to 23.9%, driven by stronger sales and lower SG&A, even as gross margin came under strain.

Segment-wise, in North America, adjusted EBITDA margin improved modestly, up 30 basis points, but internationally, margin declined by 250 basis points, reflecting unfavorable price/mix dynamics in a more competitive landscape.

Moving forward, even though the pricing is expected to be down, the raw potato costs (which account for about one-third of the company’s cost of goods sold) are also expected to be down, which should offset lower pricing to some extent. On the last earnings call, the management noted that the North American contract negotiations for the 2025 potato crop are almost done, with an expected mid-single-digit percentage decrease in price. Further, as the volume outlook is positive and sales should sequentially improve post Q4 FY2025, the company’s margin might benefit from operating leverage.

In addition, the company is taking several steps to reduce the costs. The company's restructuring plan is on track to deliver at least $55 million of pretax savings in fiscal 2025 and $85 million in fiscal 2026, with two-thirds of these savings coming from reduced selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses, and one-third from cost of goods sold [COGS]. Lamb Weston has also engaged AlixPartners, a global advisory firm, to bring into effect an "end-to-end value creation plan" that focuses on unlocking value across the entire P&L, including top-line growth, cost reductions in manufacturing, throughput, logistics, procurement, and SG&A, as well as working capital management.

The company has over 30 projects underway, targeting both quick wins and building a multi-year savings pipeline. Examples of these initiatives include rightsizing transportation modes and optimizing railcar loading in logistics, as well as executing a plan to exit surplus warehouse space.

While there are some headwinds in Q4, including temporary production curtailments to rightsize inventory levels that reduce fixed cost absorption, margins should start sequentially improving from Q1 FY2026. So, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects in the medium to long run.

Valuation And Ratings

LW stock is trading at 17.05x FY25 (ending May) consensus EPS estimate of $3.12 and 15.48x FY26 EPS estimate of $3.44, which is lower than its 5-year average forward P/E of 25.58x.

The company’s poor performance over the last couple of years explains the valuation discount versus historical levels.

However, I believe we are close to the bottom, and I see a potential for recovery with inflation coming lower than expected and consumer confidence starting to improve. The company is also doing a good job of gaining new customers and launching new products. I also see a good chance of margin improvement in FY26 as the company right-sizes its inventory in Q425 and some of its cost-saving measures start taking effect.

I see a good case for upward re-rating in the company’s valuation multiples over the coming quarters as its performance improves. Hence, I rate the stock a buy.

Risks

Global restaurant traffic is under pressure due to inflation and constrained consumer spending. Any further softness in QSR traffic due to macro factors could delay revenue recovery.

There is also FX risk with the international operations accounting for ~34% of total revenues.

Further, I am expecting the company’s good execution in terms of gaining new customers and cost reductions to continue. However, if the company is not able to perform well on these fronts, its stock price may disappoint.

data.title: Wall Street Is Missing Coinbase's Leverage To The Next Supercycle (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the last few years, we've put out a number of articles on Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN), consistently calling the stock a 'Buy':

This is due to the fact that we see the crypto space growing in importance over the next decade, as the ecosystem's overall size, functionality, and accessibility begin to rival the traditional financial system.

In the past, part of our bullish thesis has centered around Coinbase’s exposure to Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Bitcoin trading volumes, which have historically made up a large portion of the firm's revenues.

It shouldn’t be a surprise, then, that the stock has correlated strongly with Bitcoin's price since IPO:

However, as stablecoin legislation makes its way through US Congress, and we see increased interest from 'traditional' companies in the space, we think the next crypto cycle is already taking shape.

In the past, we’ve seen speculative frenzies dominated by meme coins, NFTs, and a fixation on both Bitcoin and Ethereum (ETH-USD), perhaps to the exclusion of all else.

Now, as decentralized finance apps and stablecoins begin to offer the world new payment rails for stable value exchange, we think COIN's services revenue will begin generating a majority of appreciation for shareholders going forward.

Over the last few years, Coinbase has done an excellent job at cutting costs and diversifying its revenue base. Now, with the potential for massively increased institutional adoption, we think COIN's non-transaction revenues will power the stock's next leg higher.

It’s true that COIN is expensive on some metrics, but when you break it down, we actually think the market is asking a reasonable price from investors.

Today, we'll dive into the company's recent results, explain why we think COIN's diversified revenue should bolster margins and growth, and reiterate the case that we think Coinbase is 'one to own' for the years ahead.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Our last coverage of Coinbase was in October of last year, and so before we dive into the recent updates that have reinforced our bullish view, it’s probably worth covering some of the recent numbers, just to get a sense of where the stock currently sits.

In Q4 of 2024, Coinbase reported revenues of $2.27 billion, which represented 138% year-over-year growth. Similarly, the company reported earnings per share of ~$4.68, which came in well above analyst estimates.

By all counts, this was a strong corner.

In Q1 of 2025, results were a bit weaker, with revenue actually down sequentially, although still up 24% YoY. EPS also missed by quite a large margin:

However, when you zoom out, much of this EPS volatility has to do with the company's 'Fair Value' mark of its crypto holdings.

If you look at Coinbase from a cash perspective, the firm appears to be in a much stronger operating position.

Once you strip out depreciation and amortization, stock-based compensation, and gains and losses from the crypto portfolio, adjusted EBITDA for Q1 of this year was down only very slightly YoY, from $1.02 billion to $930 million:

This reflects gaining momentum in the company's EBITDA, which has now — for several quarters — earned at roughly a $1 billion run rate.

Annualize this, and Coinbase as a firm — subject to some volatility and trading revenues — is producing roughly $4 billion a year in adjusted EBITDA. Not bad!

With some stocks, using adjusted EBITDA can be a tricky measure, but given that the majority of Coinbase's adjustments are correcting for crypto portfolio fluctuations, we think it’s actually a relatively fair representation of what’s going on, 'on the ground'.

Frankly, when we first started covering the stock back in May 2023, Coinbase was just turning the corner on revenue diversification and cost-cutting.

Now, two years on, the firm is pumping out solid, consistent adjusted EBITDA off the back of a diversified revenue base at the center of the regulated crypto ecosystem. We expect the company to continue making gains as institutional adoption increases.

Recently, we’ve seen a number of headlines that bolster our view that increased institutional adoption is on the way.

This week, the US Senate recently voted for and passed a bill giving much stronger legal footing to the stablecoin market. This is a big deal, especially considering the bill's bipartisan support.

Now, with the stroke of a pen, Donald Trump could turn whatever final bill lands on his desk into a cohesive outline for how modern businesses can actually use crypto payment rails and free themselves from oppressive credit card fees.

Additionally, as this legislation has advanced, we’ve seen comments from major retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN) that they’re looking into using crypto payment rails and stablecoins to reduce payment friction and increase profitability. CFOs at other companies may begin seeing the potential benefits of using stablecoins, and we expect Coinbase to garner a majority of this business.

Revenue-wise, Coinbase is well-prepared to monetize this opportunity.

The company is known for its retail and institutional trading platforms and brokerage capability, but COIN's revenue streams have now diversified to the point that in Q1 of this year, 35% of revenue came from stablecoins, blockchain rewards, interest, and subscription revenues from programs like 'Coinbase One':

As we see it, COIN has numerous levers to grow from both traditional routes and increased institutional crypto adoption, and the stock should move beyond how many currently on Wall Street see it — as an underperforming Bitcoin wrapper.

Valuation

So, COIN's financials have improved, and we think the firm has considerable upside in the event of increased crypto adoption through its service offerings.

Why does the stock look so expensive?

In short, by some metrics, COIN is trading at a heightened multiple. You can see this in the stock's price-to-sales ratio, which stands at around 11x:

There’s no doubt that this is high, both relative to the past, and nominally vs. the market.

However, if you look at the company, Coinbase sports considerable operating margins—at least once you strip out the net income fluctuations from the crypto portfolio. In Q1, the firm produced $2 billion in revenue and $930 million in adjusted EBITDA. This is a nearly 50% cash margin.

Not only that, but COIN is now producing roughly $4 billion in adjusted EBITDA, with a market cap of $75.2 billion. Broken down like this, the stock is actually trading at an 18x multiple. This appears MUCH more reasonable.

We would argue that for a company that has so much potential upside, to such a large potential shift in the way this financial system works, paying 18x adjusted EBITDA appears like an incredibly fair price. Again, if you look at Seeking Alpha's valuation grade of Coinbase, you’ll find the stock's multiples look quite high versus the financial sector:

In fact, the stock earns an 'F' rating.

That said, many of these numbers are skewed dramatically by the 'Fair Value' fluctuations. Additionally, the company's lumpy results and uncertain growth catalyst timings make the stock look expensive. If Coinbase manages to continue growing EBITDA into the future, we think a purchase today at under $300 per share could be incredibly advantageous for investors.

When you add up COIN's significant leverage to the next potential crypto cycle along with the diversity of revenue sources, high margins, and relatively attractive price point, we think the stock looks like a 'Strong Buy'.

Risks

Of course, when it comes to Coinbase, there are some risks to consider.

First and foremost, many of COIN's revenues come from transaction fees from retail users. These still make up the lion's share of revenue. This means that a protracted lack of interest in the crypto ecosystem would also likely have a negative impact on Coinbase's revenue picture.

This is what we saw in 2021.

However, management has — as we mentioned — taken steps to reduce reliance on trading revenues, which, we think, makes the stock appear more well-prepared for an inevitable future slowdown.

Additionally, many of the assets that trade on Coinbase’s platform remain without a regulatory framework. This presents a considerable regulatory risk for Coinbase. The stablecoin news is welcome but make no mistake — regulatory risk remains as Coinbase continues to offer altcoins that have (frankly) questionable status as securities.

Management has done an excellent job navigating the regulatory storm in the past, but we think that’s possible that risks here could flare back up. This would likely ding the stock, at least in the short term.

Summary

Overall, though, we think COIN is incredibly well-prepared to capitalize on the next crypto cycle in a way that it hasn’t been able to before.

With increased exposure to stablecoin revenues, reduced regulatory risk, and continued EBITDA margin strength, we think Coinbase's organic performance has a great trajectory.

Coupled with a multiple that we think appears very reasonable, it’s hard to see the stock as anything other than a 'Strong Buy'. Thus, our rating.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: Accenture Q3: DOGE Problem Gets Worse
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Global consulting heavyweight Accenture (ACN) reported the third-quarter earnings report of their FY25 fiscal year, giving investors another opportunity to observe the company’s pivot towards GenAI.

Accenture’s management started reporting on its GenAI business over the past 15 months, and the Q3 earnings report showed GenAI bookings now totaled $5.1B, now accounting for 6.4% of the company’s consolidated bookings volume of ~$80B on a TTM basis. As noted in my previous coverage on Accenture, management was heavily leaning on an acquisition-led strategy to bolster its AI business and insulate itself from the global slowdown seen in consulting and managed services.

But the Q3 report also showed the contraction in quarterly bookings volume accelerated on a y/y basis. The slowdown in federal discretionary IT spending may have picked up pace, which hit its overall bookings volume, complicating Accenture’s pivot to GenAI in a tough macro environment for the company.

In my view, Accenture is still a high-quality company with a management that is resilient enough to navigate the company away from these headwinds. Unfortunately for the mid-term, I expect Accenture’s headwinds to persist.

This leads to reiterating my Neutral rating on the company.

Balancing The AI Pivot Vs. D.O.G.E Headwinds

I have been mostly bullish on Accenture’s pivot to GenAI over the past year. But in my last report I noted how the company’s debt-fueled strategy to acquire a significant cohort of startups in the GenAI space and get quick access to the AI market would weigh the company’s margins down.

Accenture’s Q3 report revealed GenAI bookings were up 67% to $1.5B in the third quarter versus Accenture’s consolidated bookings of $19.7B, decreasing 6% y/y. I will address the company’s acceleration in consolidated bookings later, but for now, On the Q3 earnings call, management revealed:

We are a leader in Gen AI with another milestone quarter of $1.5 billion in bookings and over $700 million in revenues, bringing our Q3 year-to-date GenAI bookings to a total of $4.1 billion and revenue to $1.8 billion.

If I factor in Accenture’s Q3 revenues of $17.7B and GenAI revenues of ~$700M, this implies GenAI accounted for 4% of the company’s consolidated revenues. In my view, this is not particularly impressive, but it is an encouraging sign that Accenture’s acquisition-led GenAI pivot is beginning to yield results. Note that on a TTM basis, Accenture’s GenAI bookings volume totaled $5.1B.

Circling back to Accenture’s Q3 revenues, the company recorded revenue growth of 7.5% y/y, beating consensus estimates by $380M, or ~2.2%. The sustained revenue growth pace in Financial Services and Health Services helped in pushing revenue growth for the quarter higher and recording one of the biggest revenue beats versus consensus estimates in over 12 quarters.

However, the accelerated contraction in Accenture’s bookings indicates the company’s inflection mode is going to take longer.

Q3 consolidated bookings of $19.7B contracted by 6%, faster than the 3% bookings contraction reported in the Q2 FY25 quarter. In the previous quarter, management explained on the Q2 FY25 call that Accenture’s Federal Services unit accounted for 8% of the company’s FY24 revenues of $64.9B, so the impact to revenues would be moderate. But on the Q3 call, management explained that they would be seeing a 2% headwind to Q4 revenues, which they expect to fall in the range of $17-17.6B.

Management noted that their book-to-bill ratio slumped to 1x, signaling a rapid slowdown in the volume of new business they receive versus the revenue they are able to record. The last time Accenture noted an at-par book-to-bill ratio was in September 2023 (Q4 FY23 call), when GenAI had firmly dug its heels into upending the macro environment for consulting firms like Accenture.

Management attributed the contraction in Accenture’s business to a slowdown in new procurement as well as cancellations of projects, which is alarming and may have led to a quicker contraction of Accenture’s future business volume. The government spending efficiency goals that D.O.G.E. had set through the first few months of Trump 2.0's term have resulted in many of Accenture’s federal services projects being cancelled. On top of that, I believe that the rapid back-and-forth in geopolitical tensions and the tariffs narrative may have also kept businesses on the sidelines as they tighten up their discretionary IT budgets and wait till the air clears. This will continue to add pressure to Accenture’s business outlook, in my opinion.

Gross margins improved sequentially by 304 bp to 32.9% but were still 49 bp below gross margins of 33.4% in Q3 of last year, implying business economics were slightly lower versus the previous year. GAAP operating margins rose 40 bp to 16.8% over the same quarter last year after accounting for the business optimization costs being incurred by Accenture. But the pressures in running its business operations may have pushed management to revise their outlook for operating margins to now land at the lower end of their previous target range of 15.6%-15.7%.

Still, the company reported GAAP EPS of $3.49, beating estimates by 17 cents a share.

Switching to Accenture’s balance sheet, I see that the company continues to maintain an iron-clad capital structure with $9.6B in cash+equivalents+ST investments versus $8.2B in debt and obligations.

Due to the tough macro, the company has decided to allocate a smaller share of their cash towards acquisitions, now expected to be $1-1.5B. Instead, what I believe management is now prioritizing is buying back its own shares at a faster pace.

In the Q3 earnings report, management repurchased “6.0 million shares for a total of $1.8 billion,“ the largest stock buyback executed by Accenture over the past three FYs, in dollar terms, per my analysis. Note that Accenture still has $3.3B in its share buyback program. If the company is accelerating its pace of share buybacks, this could indicate that management is seeing more value in buying back its own shares versus acquiring startups or allocating cash towards dividends.

In my view, this should eventually result in being bullish for Accenture, so an inflection should be around the corner.

Re-assessing My PT For Accenture, After Q3 Results

At the moment, management is looking towards single-digit growth in FY26, with the macro environment continuing to put pressure on the company as it keeps attempting to navigate away from its headwinds.

Markets are pricing in 7-8% of EPS growth in FY26 on the back of revenue expectations of 6-7% in the same year, while Accenture’s stock trades at ~21x FY26 earnings.

Observing the trend of Accenture’s forward PE, I see that the stock trades at a discount to its historical average of forward PE. I believe the discount is fair. Why?

Because, historically, Accenture has been able to demonstrate 8.5-9% growth in EPS over the past five years on a CAGR basis. Given this history, Accenture has usually traded around 26x forward earnings. But if the company is unable to match up to its historical trend of EPS growth and expects earnings to grow in the 7-8% range, I believe the company should actually trade at 22-23x forward earnings.

This implies ~7% scope for expansion in forward PE, pointing to a PT of ~$305 per share.

Given the range of headwinds that I expect to persist after reviewing Accenture’s Q3 earnings report, along with marginal scope for PE expansion, I believe a Neutral rating is warranted.

Takeaway

Accenture’s Q3 report showed strong growth in earnings and revenue as GenAI bookings continued to expand its presence in Accenture’s business.

But, with the US federal government downsizing its IT budget as well as IT departments worldwide reigning in their IT discretionary expenses, Accenture will continue to see some pressure before its path to AI inflection grows clearer.

For now, I recommend staying Neutral on Accenture.

data.title: 2 REITs To Avoid (The Raised Nail Gets Hammered)
data.description:
I once heard a friend of mine say, “The raised nail gets hammered”.

He was a wise friend who had made millions of dollars investing in boring businesses that were off the radar.

Most of his net worth was in real estate, cash, and collectibles.

He never chased shiny new toys, and he drove an old, worn-out Lincoln.

I could tell by his modest lifestyle that he never borrowed money, and he was one of the most frugal mentors that I know.

Needless to say, he was the opposite of flashy, and I knew that when he said, “the raised nail gets hammered,” he meant to avoid high-risk speculative investments.

These days, many high-yielding stocks offer tempting yields, ranging from 13% to 18%.

I typically avoid these gun-slinging stocks because their yields almost always mean that investors perceive very low growth, and even worse, a possible dividend cut.

As long as I’ve been writing on Seeking Alpha, I’ve tried my best to help readers navigate away from these dangerous ideas because they ultimately blow up and cost investors dearly.

If you don’t believe me, just take a look at the chart I prepared a few weeks ago that compares high-yielding residential mortgage REITs with commercial mortgage REITs and equity REITs:

As you can see, there’s a lot of “red ink” in the residential mREIT category, and you’d have to be an extremely skilled market timer to break even owning these securities.

That’s not to say that all stocks that yield 10% or higher are unsafe; there are rare occasions in which shares may be beaten down due to an overreaction or an out-of-favor sector.

But these instances are rare.

Just take a look at the list of “sucker yield” stocks that I recommended avoiding in July 2019:

The mall REITs – Washington Prime and CBL – were yielding 28% and 30%, respectively. I was the voice of reason in August 2019 as I explained,

“Before you invest hard-earned capital into WPG, CBL, PEI, and MAC, make sure that you recognize that all four REITs (in yellow) are becoming increasingly dangerous.”

These days, I continue to police the REIT sector, looking for dangerous dividend stocks to avoid. Typically, these types of articles don’t get much fanfare; however, if I can convince one reader to avoid losing hard-earned capital, I’m happy.

So, without further ado, let’s take a look at 2 REITs worth avoiding…

Sachem Capital Corp. (SACH)

Sachem Capital is a commercial mortgage REIT that originates, services, and manages a portfolio of short-term loans secured by first mortgage liens on commercial real estate property.

The $556mm of total assets includes 226 loans, totaling $457mm net of reserves in 15 states (although the bulk of the loans – around 78% - are located in CT, NY, and FL). The average loan size is $2.1 mm, and the company has completed over 2,000 loans (worth over $1B since inception. The average yield of the portfolio is 13.1%, and the distribution by property type is as follows:

  • Residential: 59.3%
  • Commercial: 28.0%
  • Land: 6.1%
  • Mixed Use: 6.6%.

Over the last few years, the residential lending industry has faced a host of headwinds, such as elevated interest rates, a slowdown in home sales, and tariff uncertainty, and this has made Sachem’s income less predictable.

In addition, real estate construction has impacted construction costs, which has disrupted the supply chain.

Furthermore, many borrowers are unable to refinance projects with conventional bank financing, which has led to kicking the can down the road for many borrowers.

As of Q1 '25, Sachem had $124 million of non-performing loans (NPLs) net, compared to $103 million of non-performing loans net as of December 31st, 2024. And as shown below, earnings per share have continued to decline, which is the primary reason for underperformance.

In the latest quarter, Sachem generated revenue of $11.4 million, a 31% decrease from Q1 '24. This is primarily due to the cumulative effect of fewer loan originations over the past 15 months, as well as elevated levels of non-performing loans and conversion of loans through foreclosure to real estate owned.

As of Q1 '25, Sachem has $81.8 million on unpaid foreclosures and $4.3 million in real estate owned.

Sachem has attempted to mitigate risk by diversifying into other property categories as well as partnering with Shem Creek, a CRE Finance platform that provides debt capital solutions to multifamily, workforce housing.

Also, through its Urbane New Haven partnership, Sachem hopes to build a pipeline of development projects where it can better control risk and returns.

Back in November 2024, Sachem whacked its dividend by 37.5%, and even after this large cut, shares are now yielding by 18.2%.

Clearly, Mr. Market remains alarmed that Sachem is not out of the woods and another dividend cut could be in store. And based upon consensus estimates, it appears that there’s a high probability that a cut is coming.

In short, Sachem has been a train wreck, and I would avoid this one at all costs. Perhaps Ladder Capital (LADR) – yielding 8.7% - or Starwood Property Trust (STWD) – yielding 9.6% - will quench your thirst for yield.

Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND)

Gladstone Land is an externally managed farming REIT that owns 150 farms totaling 103,000 acres in 15 states. States with the highest concentration include California (63 farms), Colorado (12 farms), and Florida (20 farms).

Total U.S. farmland value is approximately $3.4 trillion, which makes the value proposition for farm consolidation extremely attractive. The other pure-play farm REIT is Farmland Partners (FPI), which is internally managed.

Since its IPO in 2013, Gladstone Land has grown its portfolio from 1,631 acres to over 103,00 acres (including 55,000 acre-feet of water assets).

In general, I don’t favor externally managed REITs, but with Gladstone Land, I understand that size matters, and currently, with a market cap of around $365 million, it doesn’t make economic sense for the company to hire its own management team. Importantly, Gladstone has an experienced management team that owns 9% of the shares in Gladstone Land.

I’ve owned Gladstone Land over the years, but currently I’m bearish given the deteriorating fundamentals.

As you can see, AFFO per share continues to sink, which has led to an elevated payout ratio, seemingly a “sucker yield.” As you can see below, using FAST Graphs, Gladstone Land's 2025 payout ratio (using analyst consensus data) is 136% and goes to 143% in 2027.

This suggests that the yield is not sustainable.

Currently, shares are trading at $10.09 (was $40.36 in April 2022) with a dividend yield of 5.6%...

I’ll put it bluntly, I would not bet the farm on Gladstone Land.

Farmland Partners (ticker FPI) looks much more attractive with a payout ratio of 80% and appetizing growth in 2026 (+13%) and 2027 (+9%) – based on analysts' consensus data.

In Conclusion

Don’t swing at every pitch…

Wait for the perfect pitch to come down the plate…

Long-term REIT investors should be looking for companies with dividends that are not just safe, but also have good growth prospects.

Always look for REITs based on the stability and predictability of their operating cash flows, rents, built-in increases, and real estate operating costs.

And you remember what my friend said, “the raised nail gets hammered.”

Happy SWAN Investing!

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
